title: Square & Cube Roots
blurb: Square roots and cube roots, perfect squares and cubes, and solving equations like x² = 49 and x³ = 8.

lessons.title: Square Roots
lessons.concreteComparison: A square rug covers $49$ square feet. How long is each side? You need the number that, multiplied by itself, gives $49$ — that's $7$, because $7^2=49$. Finding that number is taking a **square root**: $\sqrt{49}=7$. Squaring builds the area; the square root recovers the side.
lessons.objective: Understand the square root as the inverse of squaring, evaluate roots of perfect squares, and use the radical symbol correctly.
concept: **Squaring** a number multiplies it by itself: $7^2=49$. The **square root** undoes that: $\sqrt{49}=7$. The symbol $\sqrt{\ }$ is called a **radical**.
A **perfect square** is a whole number that is some whole number squared: $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144, \ldots$ Their square roots come out even.
Every positive number actually has **two** square roots — $7^2=49$ and $(-7)^2=49$. But the radical symbol $\sqrt{49}$ means the **principal** (positive) root: $7$, never $-7$.
Square roots of fractions work part by part: $\sqrt{\frac{9}{16}} = \frac{\sqrt{9}}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{3}{4}$.
lessons.keyIdea: $\sqrt{a}$ is the nonnegative number whose square is $a$: $\sqrt{49}=7$ because $7^2=49$.

facts.title: Perfect squares to 144
facts.explanation: Know these cold — roots come out even.

facts.title: Principal root
facts.explanation: The radical symbol means the positive root.

commonMistakes.correction: $\sqrt{100}=10$, not $50$ — find the number that multiplies by itself to give 100.
$\sqrt{49}=7$ only. The equation $x^2=49$ has two solutions, but the symbol $\sqrt{\ }$ names just the positive one.

steps.title: Perfect squares
beats: Spot one under a radical and the root is instant.

steps.title: Two squares, one symbol
beats: So $49$ has two square roots: $7$ and $-7$.
But the symbol $\sqrt{49}$ means the **positive** one.

steps.title: Evaluating square roots
steps.intro: Ask: what times itself gives this?

steps.title: Find the root
steps.intro: Perfect squares only — no calculator needed.

steps.title: The oldest math homework
steps.body: A Babylonian clay tablet nearly 4,000 years old (YBC 7289) shows a square with its diagonal labelled — and their estimate of $\sqrt{2}$ is accurate to six decimal places. Students were practicing square roots two millennia before algebra had symbols.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The radical symbol always means the **positive** root.

lessons.title: Cube Roots
lessons.concreteComparison: A shipping box holds $64$ cubic feet and is a perfect cube. How tall is it? You need the number that multiplies by itself **three** times to give $64$: that's $4$, since $4^3=64$. That's a **cube root**: $\sqrt[3]{64}=4$. Volume builds up in three dimensions; the cube root brings you back to the edge.
lessons.objective: Evaluate cube roots of perfect cubes — including negative numbers — and contrast them with square roots.
concept: **Cubing** uses three copies: $4^3=4\times4\times4=64$. The **cube root** undoes it: $\sqrt[3]{64}=4$. The little $3$ is the **index** of the radical.

Big difference from square roots: **negatives are allowed**. $(-2)^3=-8$, so $\sqrt[3]{-8}=-2$. A cube of a negative stays negative, so every number — positive or negative — has exactly **one** real cube root.
Compare: $\sqrt{64}=8$ but $\sqrt[3]{64}=4$. Same radicand, different index, different question.
lessons.keyIdea: $\sqrt[3]{a}$ is the number whose cube is $a$ — and unlike square roots, $a$ may be negative: $\sqrt[3]{-8}=-2$.

commonMistakes.correction: It's fine: $(-3)^3=-27$, so $\sqrt[3]{-27}=-3$. Only EVEN roots reject negatives.

steps.title: The cube root
beats: No index written means index $2$ (square root).

steps.title: Perfect cubes
beats: Far fewer than squares — easy to memorize.

steps.title: Which root?
steps.explanation: $4^3=64$. ($\sqrt{64}=8$ is the SQUARE root — watch the index.)

steps.title: Cube roots of negatives
beats: Squares can't do this: no real number squares to $-8$.

steps.title: Cube roots
steps.intro: Watch the sign and the index.

steps.title: The duplication of the cube
steps.body: Legend: the citizens of Delos asked the oracle how to stop a plague, and were told to double the volume of their cubic altar. Doubling the EDGE makes volume $8\times$ bigger — what they needed was an edge of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ times the original, a length the Greeks proved impossible to construct with compass and straightedge alone. One plague, two thousand years of mathematics.

lessons.title: Estimating Roots
lessons.concreteComparison: Your garden plot is $70$ square feet, and you want it square-ish. $\sqrt{70}$ isn't a whole number — but $8^2=64$ and $9^2=81$, so the side is between $8$ and $9$ feet, and since $70$ is closer to $64$, it's a bit over $8$. Squeezing a root between two perfect squares (or cubes) turns an ugly number into a usable estimate.
lessons.objective: Estimate square and cube roots of non-perfect squares and cubes by trapping them between consecutive integers and refining to the nearest tenth.
concept: To estimate $\sqrt{70}$: find the perfect squares around $70$. $8^2=64<70<81=9^2$, so $8<\sqrt{70}<9$.
**Refine by testing tenths**: $8.3^2=68.89$ and $8.4^2=70.56$. Since $68.89<70<70.56$, the root is between $8.3$ and $8.4$, and $70.56$ is closer — call it $\sqrt{70}\approx 8.4$.
Cube roots work the same with perfect cubes. $\sqrt[3]{100}$: since $4^3=64<100<125=5^3$, we get $4<\sqrt[3]{100}<5$.
The closer the radicand is to one of the bounding perfect powers, the closer the root lies to that end.
lessons.keyIdea: Trap the root between consecutive perfect powers, then test tenths to sharpen the estimate.

commonMistakes.text: Dividing by 2 (or 3) to estimate a root.
commonMistakes.correction: $\sqrt{70}$ is about $8.4$, nowhere near $35$ — trap it between perfect squares instead.

commonMistakes.text: Using perfect squares to trap a CUBE root.
commonMistakes.correction: For $\sqrt[3]{100}$ use cubes ($64$ and $125$), not squares — the bounds come from the same power you're undoing.

steps.title: Between two perfect squares
beats: Estimate $\sqrt{70}$: it's not a perfect square.
$70$ is nearer $64$, so the root is nearer $8$.

steps.title: Same trick, different powers
beats: $100$ is nearer $125$ — the root is nearer $5$.

steps.title: Trap and refine
steps.intro: Bound it, then test a tenth if needed.

steps.title: How calculators do it
steps.body: Your calculator doesn't “know” $\sqrt{70}$ — it runs the same squeeze you just did, only faster. Newton's method starts with a guess and improves it: average your guess with $70\div\text{guess}$, repeat. Three rounds from a guess of $8$ already gives $8.3666$, correct to four digits.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Cube roots use perfect cubes the same way.

lessons.title: Solving x² = k and x³ = k
lessons.concreteComparison: You know a square banner's area must be $49$ square feet, and you're solving for the side: $x^2=49$. Algebra says there are **two** numbers that work — $7$ and $-7$ — even though only $7$ makes sense for a banner. Equations with squares come with a $\pm$; equations with cubes don't. Knowing which is which keeps your answers complete.
lessons.objective: Solve equations of the form $x^2=k$ (two solutions, $x=\pm\sqrt{k}$) and $x^3=k$ (one solution, $x=\sqrt[3]{k}$), and know when to keep or drop the negative root.
concept: **$x^2=k$ has two solutions** (for $k>0$): $x=\sqrt{k}$ and $x=-\sqrt{k}$, written $x=\pm\sqrt{k}$. Both check: $7^2=49$ and $(-7)^2=49$.
**$x^3=k$ has exactly one real solution**: $x=\sqrt[3]{k}$. Cubing keeps the sign, so no second answer appears: $x^3=8$ gives only $x=2$, and $x^3=-8$ gives only $x=-2$.
In **word problems**, context can rule out the negative: a side length, a distance, an age must be positive. State $x=\pm$ first, then discard what the situation forbids.
If $k$ isn't a perfect square or cube, the answer stays exact in radical form: $x^2=50$ gives $x=\pm\sqrt{50}$.
lessons.keyIdea: $x^2=k \Rightarrow x=\pm\sqrt{k}$ (two answers); $x^3=k \Rightarrow x=\sqrt[3]{k}$ (one answer, sign preserved).

facts.title: Square equation
facts.explanation: Two solutions — don't lose the negative.

facts.title: Cube equation
facts.explanation: One solution — the sign survives cubing.

commonMistakes.correction: $x^3=8$ has only $x=2$; $(-2)^3=-8\neq 8$. Cubes keep the sign, so no second root.

steps.title: Square vs cube equations
steps.intro: Count the solutions before you write them.

steps.title: Solve them
steps.intro: ±  for squares; a single signed answer for cubes.

problems.prompt: A square patio has area $81$ m². Its side is…
problems.explanation: $x^2=81$ gives $x=\pm9$, but a length must be positive: $9$ m.

steps.title: Why ± matters
steps.body: In 1545 Cardano published solutions to cubic equations and stumbled onto square roots of NEGATIVE numbers along the way. Mathematicians called them “imaginary” as an insult — yet today those numbers run electrical engineering, quantum physics, and the graphics in your games. Keeping track of every root, even the weird ones, turned out to matter.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Context (lengths, distances) may discard the negative.

lessons.title: Simplifying Square Roots
lessons.concreteComparison: $\sqrt{50}$ looks like a dead end — $50$ isn't a perfect square. But $50=25\times 2$, and $25$ IS one. Pull it out: $\sqrt{50}=\sqrt{25}\cdot\sqrt{2}=5\sqrt{2}$. Same number, tidier form — like reducing a fraction, but for radicals.
lessons.objective: Use the product property $\sqrt{ab}=\sqrt{a}\cdot\sqrt{b}$ to write square roots in simplest radical form.
concept: **Product property:** $\sqrt{ab}=\sqrt{a}\cdot\sqrt{b}$. For example $\sqrt{36}=\sqrt{4\cdot 9}=\sqrt{4}\cdot\sqrt{9}=2\cdot3=6$ — both routes agree.
**To simplify:** find the largest perfect-square factor. $\sqrt{50}=\sqrt{25\cdot 2}=5\sqrt{2}$. $\sqrt{12}=\sqrt{4\cdot 3}=2\sqrt{3}$.
A root is in **simplest form** when nothing under the radical has a perfect-square factor left: $5\sqrt{2}$ is done, because $2$ has none.
The property runs both ways — you can also combine: $\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{8}=\sqrt{16}=4$.

commonMistakes.correction: False! $\sqrt{25}=5$, but $3+4=7$. The property works for MULTIPLICATION only.
$8$ still hides a $4$: $3\sqrt{8}=3\cdot2\sqrt2=6\sqrt{2}$. Hunt the LARGEST square factor.

steps.title: The product property
beats: It lets you split a radical into factors…

steps.title: No splitting over +
beats: Never split a radical across a plus sign.

steps.title: Why exact beats decimal
steps.body: Engineers and mathematicians prefer $5\sqrt2$ over $7.07$ because it's **exact** — decimals round, radicals don't. Squaring $5\sqrt2$ gives exactly $50$; squaring $7.07$ gives $49.9849$. Tiny errors compound, so the radical form survives all the way to the final step.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $\sqrt{ab}=\sqrt a\,\sqrt b$ — products only, never sums.
Simplest form leaves no square factor inside.

lessons.title: Roots in Formulas
lessons.concreteComparison: A landscaper knows a square lawn's area ($A=s^2$), a shipper knows a cubic crate's volume ($V=e^3$) — but each actually needs the **side**, not the area or volume. Real formulas constantly run backwards: from $A$ to $s=\sqrt{A}$, from $V$ to $e=\sqrt[3]{V}$. Roots are how you drive a formula in reverse.
lessons.objective: Use square and cube roots to solve for sides and edges in area and volume formulas, keeping exact radical answers when needed.
concept: **Square area:** $A=s^2$, so $s=\sqrt{A}$. A square of area $81$ m² has side $\sqrt{81}=9$ m.
**Cube volume:** $V=e^3$, so $e=\sqrt[3]{V}$. A cube of volume $216$ cm³ has edge $\sqrt[3]{216}=6$ cm.
If the number isn't a perfect power, answer exactly with a radical — side $=\sqrt{50}=5\sqrt{2}$ m — or estimate: $5\sqrt2\approx 7.1$ m.
Lengths are positive, so formulas take only the positive root — the $\pm$ from $x^2=k$ collapses to $+$.
lessons.keyIdea: Run formulas in reverse: $s=\sqrt{A}$ for squares, $e=\sqrt[3]{V}$ for cubes — positive roots only.

workedExamples.statement: A square field has area $144$ m². Find its side.
A cubic tank holds $343$ ft³. Find its edge.
A square patio has area $50$ m². Give the exact side length.

commonMistakes.text: Using a square root on a volume.
commonMistakes.correction: Volume is three-dimensional: $V=e^3$ needs the CUBE root, $e=\sqrt[3]{V}$.
The algebra gives $s=\pm\sqrt{A}$, but a side length is positive — keep only $+\sqrt{A}$.

tryIt.statement: A square sticker has area $25$ cm². Find its side.
A cube has volume $1000$ cm³. Find its edge.

steps.title: From area to side
beats: Lengths are positive, so no $\pm$ here.

steps.prompt: A square rug covers $64$ ft². Each side is…

steps.title: Solve for the side or edge
steps.intro: Match the root to the power in the formula.

A storage cube holds $512$ ft³. Find the edge.
A square courtyard has area $72$ m². Exact side?

steps.title: Formulas in reverse
steps.intro: Square root for areas, cube root for volumes.

problems.prompt: A square window has area $36$ ft². Side?

steps.title: You own the radicals
steps.body: You can evaluate, estimate, simplify, and solve with square and cube roots — forwards and backwards.

steps.title: You've finished “Square & Cube Roots”
steps.body: You can undo any square or cube: evaluate perfect roots, trap the messy ones between integers, simplify radicals, and drive area and volume formulas in reverse. Next: **Linear Equations** — multi-step solving, variables on both sides, and the strange equations with no solution at all.

practice.statement: A cube has volume $125$ in³. Find its edge.

testYourself.statement: A square field has area $200$ m². Give the exact side.
